title: Chapter Six: Iris Burns
content: He was inside the cottage.

I didn't invite him. I didn't unlock the door. But there Julian Cross stood in our kitchen like he'd materialized from the damp Blackwood air itself, leaning against the counter with the loose-hipped confidence of someone who had never been told no and wouldn't know what to do with the word if he heard it.

The cottage was small. It had always been small — two rooms downstairs, the attic above — but with Julian in it, the walls seemed to shrink. He took up more space than his body warranted. He filled the room with the smell of his cedar cologne and the cold he carried in from outside, and I hated how my pulse picked up at the sight of him before my brain could catch up and remind me why it shouldn't.
He was looking at Nadine.

My grandmother sat in her chair by the window, the same chair she'd occupied for as long as I could remember. Her hands rested on the crocheted blanket across her lap. Her eyes were open but unfocused, aimed somewhere past the rain-streaked glass, somewhere that wasn't here. She hadn't registered Julian's entrance. She rarely registered anything anymore.

Julian studied her the way he studied everything — thoroughly, without sentiment. His dark eyes moved over her like he was reading a diagram: the slack jaw, the vacant stare, the way her head listed slightly to the left. I watched his face do something complicated. First, assessment — calculating what she was, what she cost. Then dismissal — a gentle closing of the mental file, already moving on. And then, beneath both, a flicker of something that made my stomach clench. Hunger. Not for Nadine herself, but for what she represented. Frailty. Dependence. A woman who couldn't fight back.
He turned to me, and the flicker was gone, sealed behind the easy smile I'd once found charming.
"It must be hard," he said. His voice was soft. Concerned. The careful tenderness of a blade sliding between ribs. "Taking care of someone who's... not really here."
The words hung in the air between us.

I heard them. I understood them. But the part of me that should have responded — the part that would have stammered, or deflected, or swallowed the insult the way I'd swallowed so many before — that part went silent. Something else stirred. Something old. Something that had been sleeping in the walls of this cottage for a hundred and eighty-three years, curled in the beams and the mortar and the blood in my veins, and it heard what Julian said, and it *woke up*.
Something cracked inside me.

Not like a bone. Not like ice on a winter pond. Like a dam. Like a wall built by generations of women who'd been told to be quiet, be small, be grateful, be good — and the wall had held, it had held for so long, but the water behind it had been rising since 1843 and the pressure was biblical and the crack ran through me like lightning and the rage that poured through it was not mine alone.
It was Iris's.
My hands began to glow.

Blue flame — not orange, not yellow, not any color fire had ever been in the natural world — threaded between my fingers like luminous veins. It licked across my knuckles and pooled in my palms, and I felt the heat of it, a dry high-desert heat that should have burned but instead felt like recognition. Like coming home to a house I'd never seen but had always known was waiting.
I stared at my own hands. At the fire that shouldn't exist. At the impossible, terrifying, *beautiful* light pouring off my skin.
"Get out," I said.

The voice that came from my throat was not mine. It was deeper. Older. It resonated in my chest like a struck bell, and the sound of it — rough, burning, absolute — frightened me more than the fire. I didn't know who was speaking. I only knew that she meant it with every molecule of her ancient, furious being.

Julian didn't move. He raised an eyebrow — amused, the way he was always amused, the way he'd been amused by everything since the day I met him, because Julian Cross found the entire world vaguely entertaining and nothing in it truly dangerous.
The fire didn't care what he found amusing.
The power erupted.

Every candle in the cottage — the three on the mantle, the two on the kitchen table, the stub Nadine never let me throw away on the windowsill — ignited at once. Blue flames burst from their wicks like small screams. Fire ran along the baseboards in bright lines, tracing the perimeter of the room in a circuit of light, and I could smell it — burning wood and hot dust and something underneath that was older than both, a char that tasted like generations. The window behind Nadine's chair cracked. Not a sharp sound but a deep, resonant *groan*, the glass splintering into a spiderweb of fractures that caught the blue light and fractured it further, throwing wild patterns on the walls.
And then the sketch.

Julian had brought it. That was why he'd come. He was holding it — a charcoal drawing on heavy cream paper, his sketch of me from the night we'd met at the bonfire. He'd drawn me like he saw me, or like he wanted to see me: softer, smaller, mouth half-open, eyes vague and admiring. A portrait of a girl who didn't know her own worth yet. A girl who might be grateful.

The blue flame found it the way fire finds kindling — inevitable, hungry, almost joyful. The sketch caught from the edge first, the paper curling black, and then the whole thing went up in a rush that took less than three seconds. The charcoal lines of my face twisted and shriveled and were gone. Ash drifted to the floor like dark snow. The air filled with the smell of burning paper — acrid, sharp, almost sweet at the edges.
Julian's mask dropped.

I'd never seen it happen before. I hadn't even known it was a mask — I'd thought his smooth expressions, his controlled half-smile, his calm dark gaze were just who he was. But in the blue firelight I saw the exact instant it fell away, and beneath it was something raw and terrible. Surprise. Not the performed kind — the real kind. His eyes went wide. His jaw tightened until I could see the tendon stand out in his neck. His beautiful face twisted into something ugly, something that looked almost like — fear.
One second. That's all I got. One second of the truth behind Julian Cross.
Then the mask went back up, smooth as water closing over a stone.
"You're..." He exhaled. His voice was barely a whisper. "Magnificent."

I wanted him to be angry. I wanted him to shout, to rage, to show me the monster I'd always sensed coiled beneath his charm. Instead he looked at me like I was a sunrise he hadn't expected. Like I was something to possess.
That was worse. That was so much worse.
I ran.

Up the narrow stairs, the attic steps groaning under my feet, the blue fire still flickering on my fingertips. I didn't look back at Julian. I couldn't look at Nadine. I couldn't be in that room one more second with the fire pouring out of me and his hungry eyes drinking it in.

The attic was dark and cold and smelled like dust and old paper, and it was the only place I ever felt safe. I stumbled to the box of Iris's things — I knew where it was now, could find it blind — and my glowing hands reached inside without hesitation. The journals. I found the one I needed by feel. It ran hot, even through the leather cover, hotter than any of the others, hot like a coal someone had forgotten to douse.
I pulled it out and it fell open in my lap, pages flexing, and the book knew — somehow the book knew — exactly where I needed to be. A single page in the middle. A single entry.
**BURN IT ALL.**

Three words. The only three words Iris Buena ever published. I'd heard the story — everyone in Blackwood Vale had. The broadside that appeared overnight on the church door in 1843, printed in black ink that was never satisfactorily explained, the three words that scandalized the town and cemented Iris's reputation as the madwoman of the vale. But no one ever knew what it meant. No one ever knew why.
Below the large words, written smaller in Iris's cramped, fierce handwriting:
*They called my grief hysteria. My fury, madness. My love, disease. Burn it. Burn it all. I will not be ashamed of my fire.*
I read the words and the blue flame on my hands blazed brighter, and I understood.
Rage Fire. Not a spell. Not a technique to be learned through practice and discipline, the way Maren tried to teach me. It was a permission. A granting. An unashaming.

Iris had been too much. That's what they told her — the men in black coats with their Latin diagnoses and their pitying smiles and their hands that patted and prodded and prescribed fresh air and rest. She'd held a child she couldn't keep — I saw it now, a flash of memory not my own, a tiny red face screwed up with crying, and Iris's arms around it, and the warmth of new life against her chest, and then the aching empty where the child had been when they took it away. They told her her sadness was a sickness. They told her her anger was proof of her instability. They told her she was broken, and they prescribed her silence, and she took it, she swallowed it, she held it in her body like swallowed glass, and it cut her from the inside for a hundred and eighty-three years.
Until now.

The blue fire surged. Not out of control — *into* control. I felt Iris's power flood through me like a river finding its course after a long drought. The heat in my palms stabilized. The trembling in my hands went still. The flame settled into a steady burn, and I knew — not with my mind but with my blood, my bones, the ancient cells of me — that I could call this fire whenever I needed it. Not by thinking. By feeling. By allowing the rage and the grief to move through me instead of locking them away.

Iris burned with a fury that had nowhere to go. The women of Blackwood Vale had been taught to swallow their fire for six generations — each one smaller and quieter than the last, each one more afraid of the heat inside her, until Nadine couldn't even look at a candle flame without her hands shaking, until I didn't know the fire was there at all.
But Iris kept it alive. In the walls. In the blood. In the hot journal and the three words on the church door and the rage that outlasted every man who'd tried to silence it.

I closed my eyes, and for one moment I felt her — not a ghost, not a presence, but a *legacy*. Iris Buena had been too much, and she refused to be ashamed, and she'd waited a hundred and eighty-three years for someone to carry what she couldn't.
I carried it now.

The flame on my hands dimmed to a whisper, then went out. The attic was dark again. My fingers trembled against the journal's warm leather. The taste in my mouth was salt and copper — tears and blood, mine and not-mine, the flavor of a rage so old it had become mineral.
I sat in the dark for a long time before I went back downstairs.

The cottage was quiet. The candles had burned to their normal flames — small, orange, ordinary. The blue fire had left no scorch marks on the baseboards. The cracked window held its spiderweb pattern but the glass was stable, the fractures frozen as if the cold had sealed them. The only damage was a small pile of gray ash on the floor where Julian had been standing. His sketch. Gone. Every trace of it reduced to powder and memory.
Julian was gone.

I found Nadine exactly where I'd left her — in her chair by the window. But she wasn't the same. She was sitting up. Straighter than I'd seen her sit in years, her spine aligned, her shoulders back, her head level. Her eyes were clear. Not the distant, foggy gaze I was used to — these were my grandmother's eyes, dark and sharp and fully present, looking at me with something I'd never seen directed at me before.
Recognition.
"That was her," Nadine said. Her voice was rough from disuse but steady. "That was Iris."

I stood in the kitchen doorway, my hands gripped together, the ghost-warmth still humming in my fingertips. I didn't know what to say. I didn't know if what had just happened was a miracle or a catastrophe or both.

"I could never do that," Nadine continued. She wasn't looking at the window or the ash or the candles. She was looking at me. Really looking. "I was too afraid of my own fire." Her voice cracked on the word *afraid*, and I heard in it a lifetime of swallowed flames, of hands kept under tables, of nights spent telling herself that the flickering light behind her ribs was just indigestion, just anxiety, just her being too much again.
She looked at me, and her eyes were wet, and her voice went quiet.
"Don't be afraid of yours."

The words hit me like the fire had — sudden, hot, impossible to contain. Something cracked again, but this time it wasn't rage. It was the thing I'd been holding even tighter than the anger, the thing I'd buried deeper because hoping hurt more than burning. My throat closed. My eyes welled. My grandmother — the woman who hadn't spoken a full sentence in months, who stared through windows and forgot my name and existed in a fog I couldn't reach — my grandmother was *here*. She saw me. She saw what I'd done, the fire and the fury and the destruction, and she wasn't afraid.
She was proud.
I crossed the kitchen and knelt beside her chair. I took her hand — her real, warm, present hand — and pressed it between my palms. She squeezed back. Her grip was stronger than I expected.
"I won't," I said.

Outside, the rain had stopped. Through the cracked window I could smell wet earth and green things growing and the particular char that lingered after lightning — or something like it. My fingers were still warm. The fire was banked but alive, coiled somewhere beneath my skin, patient as the woman who'd kept it for a century and a half.
I had Iris's rage now. I had her flame. And Julian Cross — beautiful, untouchable, hungry Julian — had looked at me burning and called me magnificent.
He meant it as a compliment. He meant it as a claim.
He was wrong. The fire wasn't for him. It was never for him.
It was for me.
